content:
4 min read
Just now

--

When I first started building AI agents, I thought intelligence would be the hardest problem to solve.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Berke Citak on UnsplashIf I picked the right model, wrote good prompts, and gave it enough tools, I assumed everything else would fall into place.
The demos certainly made it look that way.
Agents were writing code.
Browsing websites.
Researching topics.
Planning tasks.
Using APIs.
Even completing multi-step workflows with minimal human input.
It looked like intelligence had finally become a software feature.
So I spent the next several months building AI agents for coding, research, automation, document processing, and internal workflows.
Some of them were genuinely impressive.
Others failed in ways that no demo video ever mentioned.
That’s when I realized something surprising.
The more I worked with AI agents, the less I thought of them as “smart.”
Not because they weren’t capable.
But because I finally understood what actually makes an AI agent successful.
Here are the nine lessons that completely changed my perspective.
1. Intelligence Isn’t the Biggest Challenge
When an AI agent fails, most people assume the model wasn’t smart enough.
That hasn’t been my experience.
Most failures come from poor workflows.
Missing context.
Weak tool integration.
Ambiguous instructions.
Broken APIs.
Poor error handling.
The model often isn’t the weakest link.
The surrounding software is.
2. Context Beats Capability
I’ve watched smaller models outperform larger ones simply because they had better context.
A clear objective.
Relevant documentation.
Good examples.
Well-defined constraints.
The difference was dramatic.
An AI agent doesn’t magically know what success looks like.
You have to define it.
The better the context, the better the decisions.
3. More Tools Usually Mean More Confusion
One of my earliest mistakes was giving agents every tool I could think of.
Web search.
Database access.
Python execution.
Email.
Browser automation.
Multiple APIs.
It sounded powerful.
Instead, the agent spent more time deciding which tool to use than actually solving the problem.
Now I do the opposite.
Each agent has one clear job and only the tools required to complete it.
Performance improved almost immediately.
4. Reliability Is More Valuable Than Brilliance
A brilliant answer now and then isn’t enough.
Especially if you’re building production software.
I’d rather have an agent that’s correct 98% of the time than one that’s occasionally spectacular but frequently unpredictable.
Reliability builds trust.
Trust is what makes automation useful.
5. The Workflow Matters More Than the Prompt
Prompt engineering gets most of the attention.
Workflow engineering deserves far more.
The best agents I’ve built don’t rely on one giant prompt.
They follow structured steps.
Gather information.
Validate results.
Recover from failures.
Know when to stop.
The prompt starts the process.
The workflow determines the outcome.
6. AI Agents Still Need Software Engineering
This was probably the biggest surprise.
Building AI agents felt remarkably similar to building traditional software.
I still needed logging.
Monitoring.
Version control.
Testing.
Retries.
Security.
Deployment.
Documentation.
The language model was simply another component in the architecture.
The engineering principles never disappeared.
7. Good Agents Know Their Limits
One lesson I learned quickly was that agents shouldn’t try to solve everything.
The best systems know when to ask for clarification.
When to stop.
When to hand control back to the user.
When to avoid making assumptions.
Boundaries make agents feel more dependable, not less capable.
8. Debugging Became More Important Than Prompting
Early on, whenever an agent failed, I rewrote the prompt.
Now I investigate the system.
What context did it receive?
Which tool failed?
Was the retrieved information correct?
Did the workflow skip a step?
Most improvements came from debugging the pipeline rather than rewriting instructions.
That’s a very software-engineering problem.
9. AI Agents Aren’t Replacing Developers
They’re changing what developers spend time doing.
I write less boilerplate.
Less repetitive code.
Fewer one-off scripts.
Instead, I spend more time designing workflows.
Reviewing outputs.
Defining constraints.
Connecting systems.
Making architectural decisions.
In other words, AI didn’t eliminate my job.
It automated the parts of my job that were easiest to automate.
The Biggest Lesson
I used to think AI agents succeeded because they were intelligent.
Now I think they succeed because they’re built on well-designed systems.
Good architecture.
Reliable infrastructure.
Clear workflows.
Useful tools.
High-quality context.
Thoughtful constraints.
Those factors consistently matter more than chasing the newest model release.
The model is important.
The system around it is what makes it useful.
Final Thoughts
The more I build AI agents, the less interested I become in asking,
“How smart is this model?”
Instead, I ask questions like:

• Does it have the right content?

• Can I trust its workflow?

• Will it recover gracefully when something goes wrong?

• Is the system predictable enough for real work?
Those questions have made me build better AI systems than any prompt trick ever has.
AI agents are incredibly capable.
But after working with them every week, I’ve stopped thinking of them as magical, all-knowing assistants.
I see them for what they really are:
Powerful software systems that perform best when they’re supported by thoughtful engineering.
And once I understood that, everything about building AI agents started making a lot more sense.
